The ReQAP System for Question Answering.author: Philipp Christmann and Gerhard Weikum
The ReQAP System for Question Answering.title: The ReQAP System for Question Answering over Personal Information
The ReQAP System for Question Answering.abstract: Personal information is abundant on users' devices, from structured data in calendar, shopping records or fitness tools, to unstructured contents in mail and social media posts. This works presents the ReQAP system that supports users with answers for complex questions that involve filters, joins and aggregation over heterogeneous sources. The unique trait of ReQAP is that it recursively decomposes questions and incrementally builds an operator tree for execution. Both the question interpretation and the individual operators make smart use of light-weight language models, with judicious fine-tuning. The demo showcases the rich functionality for advanced user questions, and also offers detailed tracking of how the answers are computed by the operators in the execution tree. Being able to trace answers back to the underlying sources is vital for human comprehensibility and user trust in the system.
The ReQAP System for Question Answering.keywords: Question answering, Computer science, Personally identifiable information, Questions and answers, Information retrieval, Information system, World Wide Web, Action (physics), Internet privacy, Personal information management, The Internet, Closed-ended question, Term (time), Psychology

Large Language Model Enhancement Through.author: Zheng Zhou and Wei Xie and Huifang Xu and Panfei Liang and Yanqing Yang
Large Language Model Enhancement Through.title: Large Language Model Enhancement Through Multi-Domain Knowledge Integration and Semantic Retrieval in Power Systems
Large Language Model Enhancement Through.abstract: With the ongoing digital transformation across the power sector, large language models (LLMs) are increasingly expected to handle domain-specific tasks involving heterogeneous databases and technical vocabularies. To address this demand, we present KARF-Power, a novel framework that combines natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) generation with retrieval-augmented inference across multi-source power-related knowledge bases. Specifically, the framework translates natural language queries into structured database commands while leveraging text vectorization and semantic indexing to retrieve relevant context from specialized corpora such as marketing records and equipment management logs. The retrieved knowledge is then integrated into the LLM's decoding process, enabling context-aware response generation for downstream tasks such as multi-label classification and cross-database querying. This hybrid approach improves interpretability, enhances domain adaptation, and provides a scalable pathway for embedding expert knowledge into LLM-driven applications in the power domain. Extensive experiments on simulated enterprise-level datasets confirm the practical feasibility of our method, showcasing its potential to support real-time intelligent decision-making across diverse business scenarios.

AI-Augmented SOC: A Survey of LLMs and A.author: S. Srinivas and Brandon Kirk and Julissa Zendejas and Michael Espino and Matthew Boskovich and Abdul Bari and Khalil Dajani and Nabeel Alzahrani
AI-Augmented SOC: A Survey of LLMs and A.title: AI-Augmented SOC: A Survey of LLMs and Agents for Security Automation
AI-Augmented SOC: A Survey of LLMs and A.abstract: The increasing volume, velocity, and sophistication of cyber threats have placed immense pressure on modern Security Operations Centers (SOCs). Traditional rule-based and manual processes are proving insufficient, leading to alert fatigue, delayed responses, high false-positive rates, analyst dependency, and escalating operational costs. Recent advancements in Artificial Intelligence (AI) offer new opportunities to transform SOC workflows through automation and augmentation. Large Language Models (LLMs) and autonomous AI agents have shown strong potential in enhancing capabilities such as log summarization, alert triage, threat intelligence, incident response, report generation, asset discovery, and vulnerability management. This paper reviews recent developments in the application of LLMs and AI agents across these SOC functions, introducing a taxonomy that organizes their roles and capabilities within operational pipelines. While these technologies improve detection accuracy, response time, and analyst support, challenges persist, including model interpretability, adversarial robustness, integration with legacy systems, and the risk of hallucinations or data leakage. A detailed capability-maturity model outlines the levels of integration with SOC tasks. This survey synthesizes trends, identifies persistent limitations, and outlines future directions for trustworthy, explainable, and safe AI integration in SOC environments.
AI-Augmented SOC: A Survey of LLMs and A.keywords: Sophistication, Computer security, Workflow, Computer science, Automation, Risk analysis (engineering), Asset (computer security), Vulnerability (computing), Adversarial system, Taxonomy (biology), Engineering, Situation awareness, Process management, Knowledge management, Data science, System integration, Data integration

TCSR‐SQL: Towards Table Content‐Aware Te.author: Wenbo Xu and Yan Liang and Chuanyi Liu and Peiyi Han and Haifeng Zhu and Yong Xu and Yingwei Liang and Bob Zhang
TCSR‐SQL: Towards Table Content‐Aware Te.title: TCSR‐SQL: Towards Table Content‐Aware Text‐to‐SQL With Self‐Retrieval
TCSR‐SQL: Towards Table Content‐Aware Te.abstract: ABSTRACT Large language model‐based (LLM‐based) text‐to‐SQL methods have achieved important progress in generating SQL queries for real‐world applications. When confronted with table content‐aware questions in real‐world scenarios, ambiguous data content keywords and nonexistent database schema column names within the question lead to the poor performance of existing methods. To solve this problem, we propose a novel approach towards table content‐aware text‐to‐SQL with self‐retrieval (TCSR‐SQL). It leverages LLM's in‐context learning capability to extract data content keywords within the question and infer possible related database schema, which is used to generate Seed SQL to fuzz search databases. The search results are further used to confirm the encoding knowledge with the designed encoding knowledge table, including column names and exact stored content values used in the SQL. The encoding knowledge is sent to obtain the final Precise SQL following multi‐rounds of generation‐execution‐revision process. To validate our approach, we introduce a table‐content‐aware, question‐related benchmark dataset, containing 2115 question‐SQL pairs. Comprehensive experiments conducted on this benchmark demonstrate the remarkable performance of TCSR‐SQL, achieving an improvement of at least 27.8% in execution accuracy compared to other state‐of‐the‐art methods.
TCSR‐SQL: Towards Table Content‐Aware Te.keywords: Computer science, SQL, Table (database), Column (typography), Benchmark (surveying), Schema (genetic algorithms), Encoding (memory), Data mining, Information retrieval, Database, Relational database, Database index, Knowledge extraction, Row, Knowledge base, Performance improvement, Artificial intelligence, Execution time, Database schema, Table of contents, Data modeling, Query by Example, Relational database management system, Natural language processing, Programming language

Adobe Summit Concierge Evaluation with H.author: Yiru Chen and Sally Fang and Sai Sree Harsha and Dan Luo and Vaishnavi Muppala and Fei Wu and Jiang, Shun and Kun Qian and Yunyao Li
Adobe Summit Concierge Evaluation with H.title: Adobe Summit Concierge Evaluation with Human in the Loop
Adobe Summit Concierge Evaluation with H.abstract: Generative AI assistants offer significant potential to enhance productivity, streamline information access, and improve user experience in enterprise contexts. In this work, we present Summit Concierge, a domain-specific AI assistant developed for Adobe Summit. The assistant handles a wide range of event-related queries and operates under real-world constraints such as data sparsity, quality assurance, and rapid deployment. To address these challenges, we adopt a human-in-the-loop development workflow that combines prompt engineering, retrieval grounding, and lightweight human validation. We describe the system architecture, development process, and real-world deployment outcomes. Our experience shows that agile, feedback-driven development enables scalable and reliable AI assistants, even in cold-start scenarios.
Adobe Summit Concierge Evaluation with H.keywords: Workflow, Summit, Software deployment, Engineering, Scalability, Software engineering, Computer science, Quality (philosophy), Human-in-the-loop, Systems engineering, Adobe, World Wide Web, FLEX, Multimedia, Key (lock), Point (geometry)

Benchmarking Geospatial Question Answeri.author: Zekun Li and Malcolm Grossman and Ehsan Qasemi and Mihir Kulkarni and Muhao Chen and Yao-Yi Chiang
Benchmarking Geospatial Question Answeri.title: Benchmarking Geospatial Question Answering with MapQA
Benchmarking Geospatial Question Answeri.abstract: Geospatial question answering (QA) is a fundamental task in navigation and point of interest (POI) searches, yet existing datasets are limited in scale, diversity, and they rely on text-only descriptions without incorporating geometries. We introduce MapQA, a dataset that couples question-answer pairs with geo-entity geometries from OpenStreetMap (OSM) across two regions (Southern California and Illinois). MapQA contains 3,154 QA pairs covering nine geospatial reasoning types, including neighborhood inference and type identification, expanding both the quantity and variety of existing resources. To evaluate methods, we compare (1) a retrieval-based model that ranks geo-entities by embedding similarity and (2) large language models (LLMs) that translate questions into SQL queries executed on OSM. Retrieval-based models capture spatial relations like closeness and direction but fail on explicit distance computations, while LLMs excel at one-hop reasoning yet struggle with multi-hop tasks, revealing a key challenge for future systems. MapQA is publicly available at https://github.com/knowledge-computing/MapQA-dataset.
Benchmarking Geospatial Question Answeri.keywords: Geospatial analysis, Computer science, Variety (cybernetics), Benchmarking, Inference, Closeness, Question answering, Information retrieval, Key (lock), Point (geometry), Similarity (geometry), Task (project management), Embedding, SQL, Data science, Data mining, Spatial database, Semantics (computer science), Artificial intelligence, Point of interest, Spatial query, Spatial intelligence, Signature (topology), Query language, Dimension (graph theory)

RailEstate: An Interactive System for Me.author: Chen-Wei Chang and Yu‐Chieh Cheng and Yun-En Tsai and Fanglan Chen and Chang‐Tien Lu
RailEstate: An Interactive System for Me.title: RailEstate: An Interactive System for Metro Linked Property Trends
RailEstate: An Interactive System for Me.abstract: Access to metro systems plays a critical role in shaping urban housing markets by enhancing neighborhood accessibility and driving property demand. We present RailEstate, a novel web-based system that integrates spatial analytics, natural language interfaces, and interactive forecasting to analyze how proximity to metro stations influences residential property prices in the Washington metropolitan area. Unlike static mapping tools or generic listing platforms, RailEstate combines 25 years of historical housing data with transit infrastructure to support low-latency geospatial queries, time-series visualizations, and predictive modeling. Users can interactively explore ZIP-code-level price patterns, investigate long-term trends, and forecast future housing values around any metro station. A key innovation is our natural language chatbot, which translates plain-English questions (e.g., "What is the highest price in Falls Church in the year 2000?") into executable SQL over a spatial database. This unified and interactive platform empowers urban planners, investors, and residents to derive actionable insights from metro-linked housing data—without requiring technical expertise. A demonstration video of the system is available at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZLiz8S1UXsc.
RailEstate: An Interactive System for Me.keywords: Metropolitan area, Executable, Geospatial analysis, Property (philosophy), Computer science, Key (lock), Listing (finance), SQL, Real estate, Transport engineering, Land use, Spatial analysis, Property management, Database, Computer security, Natural (archaeology), Bundle, Data science, Transit-oriented development, Urban planning, Visualization, Natural language, Natural landscape, Geographic information system, Digital mapping, Residential property, Global city, Real property, Data visualization

Spatial Pattern Search through Language.author: Nicole R Schneider
Spatial Pattern Search through Language.title: Spatial Pattern Search through Language Models
Spatial Pattern Search through Language.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently begun to excel at complex domain-specific questions thanks to techniques like Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). However, the spatiotemporal reasoning abilities of LLMs are still lacking. Spatial reasoning is required to answer questions in a variety of domains that are grounded in the physical world, including urban planning, civil engineering, travel, and others. To improve LLMs and facilitate an impact in these domains, we aim to enable LLMs to reason over spatial data. In this paper we outline the challenges associated with spatial reasoning through LLMs and describe a framework to integrate spatial data with LLMs which will allow them to answer complex spatial questions.
Spatial Pattern Search through Language.keywords: Computer science, Variety (cybernetics), Spatial intelligence, Artificial intelligence, Data science, Spatial analysis, Spatial ecology, Natural language processing, Pattern language (formal languages), Field (mathematics), Spatial ability, Visual reasoning

Construction of Spinning Frame Operation.author: Siyuan Li and Yiping Yuan and Min Li
Construction of Spinning Frame Operation.title: Construction of Spinning Frame Operation and Maintenance Knowledge Graph Based on BiLSTM-CRF-BERT
Construction of Spinning Frame Operation.abstract: Abstract To enhance the rapid fault-source localization capability and improve the precision of maintenance strategy delivery during O&M processes of spinning frames, thereby addressing the issues of high dependency on manual expertise and low knowledge reuse rate prevalent in spinning workshops, this paper proposes a novel method for constructing a Knowledge Graph for spinning frame O&M based on a BiLSTM-CRF-BERT framework. Firstly, a domain-adapted and optimized BiLSTM-CRF model is employed to achieve high-precision Named Entity Recognition. This step facilitates the standardized definition of five core entity categories: fault types, fault locations, fault phenomena, cause analysis, and solution methods. Secondly, a dual-path relationship extraction model integrating rule-based approaches and BERT is designed. This model utilizes rule-based pattern matching for structured data and leverages BERT for semantic parsing of unstructured text, enabling efficient extraction of semantic relationships between entities. Finally, the spinning frame O&M Knowledge Graph is constructed using the Neo4j graph database, supporting functionalities including visualized fault information retrieval, dynamic interaction, and intelligent maintenance strategy guidance. Experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of this method in both entity recognition and relationship extraction tasks, effectively reducing fault diagnosis time and lowering O&M costs.

NL2MCP: A New Framework for Natural Lang.author: Won-Bae Kim and Nammee Moon
NL2MCP: A New Framework for Natural Lang.booktitle: Journal of the Korea Society of Computer and Information
NL2MCP: A New Framework for Natural Lang.title: NL2MCP: A New Framework for Natural Language Query Execution
NL2MCP: A New Framework for Natural Lang.abstract: 본 연구는 자연어 질의를 데이터베이스 실행으로 변환하는 두 가지 접근, NL2SQL과 NL2MCP를 동일 조건에서 비교·분석하였다. 언어모델은 GPT-4o, 데이터베이스는 PostgreSQL, 데이터셋은 Spider dev를 사용하고, NL2SQL, NL2MCP(cache), NL2MCP(live) 세 경로를 설정하였다. 실험은 정확도(EM), 실행 성공률(EX), 평균 및 p95 지연, 오류율을 난이도별(easy, medium, hard, extra)로 측정하였다. 결과적으로 NL2MCP는 EM에서는 낮았으나 EX와 지연 안정성에서 우위를 보였으며, 특히 live 모드는 스키마 불일치 오류를 줄이고 hard·extra 구간에서 가장 높은 완수율을 달성하였다. 이는 실제 운영 환경에서 NL2MCP(live)가 가장 신뢰할 수 있는 실행 경로임을 보여준다.
NL2MCP: A New Framework for Natural Lang.keywords: Computer science, Query language, RDF query language, Programming language, Natural language, Query optimization, Spider, Natural language processing, Artificial intelligence, Object Query Language, Information retrieval, Data control language, Natural (archaeology), Web search query, Natural language user interface, World Wide Web, Query expansion, Database query, Natural language generation, Data manipulation language, Database, Web query classification

NL2SQL Method in the Power Domain Based.author: Binhao Huang and Jinhui Zhang and Yayun Sun and Wang Luo and Yuan Guo and Shijie Hu and Kang Wang and Yuxuan Zhao
NL2SQL Method in the Power Domain Based.title: NL2SQL Method in the Power Domain Based on the Multi-Layer Collaborative Optimization Strategy
NL2SQL Method in the Power Domain Based.abstract: The rapid advancement of large language models has partially addressed semantic comprehension and contextual ambiguity in NL2SQL (Natural Language to SQL). However, critical challenges persist within specialized domains such as power systems, including human-machine cognitive gaps, metadata silos, and heterogeneity in SQL dialects. To mitigate these issues, we propose a multi-layer cooperative optimization framework for domain-specific NL2SQL in the power industry.At the semantic Layer: A dynamic knowledge repository of power industry entities is constructed to map domain-specific concepts to business logic, resolving ambiguities in operational intent and metric definitions.At the logical Layer: Field-level lineage mapping interconnects metadata silos with business entities, establishing traceable data provenance.At the physical Layer: A self-adaptive compilation architecture enables modular cascade compilation across diverse SQL dialects via intermediate transformation tools.Ultimately, domain entity recognition models and vector search engines integrate alignment outcomes from the semantic and logical layers. Historical SQL queries retrieved through vector search serve as few-shot learning exemplars, generating multi-prompt ensembles. This approach delivers a flexible, high-accuracy NL2SQL solution adaptable to typical power system scenarios.
NL2SQL Method in the Power Domain Based.keywords: Computer science, Metadata, Ambiguity, Domain (mathematical analysis), SQL, Modular design, Software engineering, Data modeling, Data integration, Domain-specific language, Data integrity, Matching (statistics), Semantic matching, Logical data model, Hierarchy, Database, Information retrieval, Data mining, Query language, Metric (unit), Prolog, Business rule, Metadata modeling, Class hierarchy, Programming language, Semantic Web, Data mapping, Logical conjunction, Data model (GIS), Data management, Personalization, Search engine indexing, Architecture

ASASQL: Active Data Synthesis and Annota.author: Rui Zheng and Zhiyuan Wu and Shengbing Shi and Qiang Bai and Hao Li and Yu Hui and Weigang Wu
ASASQL: Active Data Synthesis and Annota.title: ASASQL: Active Data Synthesis and Annotation for Efficient Natural Language SQL Generation in Cloud Systems
ASASQL: Active Data Synthesis and Annota.abstract: Modern cloud service providers increasingly rely on extensive natural language database queries for daily operational workflows, including system maintenance, fault recovery, and customer ticket resolution, all of which are critical for ensuring optimal data availability, service reliability, and customer experience. However, the specialized nature of cloud platform data inherently complicates this task: current practices either depend heavily on Site Reliability Engineer (SRE) experts for manual SQL query or require intensive data collection and manual annotation to train domain-specific Natural Language to SQL (NL2SQL) models for automation-both approaches suffering from high labour costs and limited scalability. To address these challenges, this paper introduces ASASQL, Active Learning-based Data Synthesis and Annotation Strategy for SQL Generation, a novel framework designed to automatically generate training data and select data for annotation to train domain-specific nl2sql models. ASASQL significantly reduces the annotation requirement for training accurate, cost-efficient, and scalable automatic data querying models. Experiments on realworld production datasets from a major cloud service provider demonstrate that ASASQL can reduce the amount of data annotation needed to achieve specific accuracy by $38.7 \%$.
ASASQL: Active Data Synthesis and Annota.keywords: Computer science, SQL, Cloud computing, Annotation, Scalability, Database, Service (business), Data modeling, Service provider, Natural language, Data mining, Reliability (semiconductor), Information retrieval, Cloud database, Data retrieval, Big data, Data integrity, Data definition language, Ticket, Relational database, Data integration, Distributed database, Database schema, Query language

DeepEye-SQL: A Software-Engineering-Insp.author: Boyan Li and Chong Chen and Xue, Zhujun and Mei, Yinan and Yuyu Luo
DeepEye-SQL: A Software-Engineering-Insp.title: DeepEye-SQL: A Software-Engineering-Inspired Text-to-SQL Framework
DeepEye-SQL: A Software-Engineering-Insp.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced Text-to-SQL, yet existing solutions still fall short of system-level reliability. The limitation is not merely in individual modules -- e.g., schema linking, reasoning, and verification -- but more critically in the lack of structured orchestration that enforces correctness across the entire workflow. This gap motivates a paradigm shift: treating Text-to-SQL not as free-form language generation but as a software-engineering problem that demands structured, verifiable orchestration. We present DeepEye-SQL, a software-engineering-inspired framework that reframes Text-to-SQL as the development of a small software program, executed through a verifiable process guided by the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). DeepEye-SQL integrates four synergistic stages: it grounds user intent through robust schema linking, enforcing relational closure; enhances fault tolerance with N-version SQL generation; ensures deterministic verification via a ``Syntax-Logic-Quality'' tool-chain that intercepts errors pre-execution; and introduces confidence-aware selection that leverages execution-guided adjudication to resolve ambiguity beyond simple majority voting. Leveraging open-source MoE LLMs (~30B total, ~3B activated parameters) without any fine-tuning, DeepEye-SQL achieves 73.5% execution accuracy on BIRD-Dev, 75.07% on the official BIRD-Test leaderboard, and 89.8% on Spider-Test, outperforming state-of-the-art solutions that rely on larger models or extensive training. This highlights that principled orchestration, rather than LLM scaling alone, is key to achieving system-level reliability in Text-to-SQL.
DeepEye-SQL: A Software-Engineering-Insp.keywords: Computer science, Correctness, Verifiable secret sharing, Workflow, Software engineering, Programming language, Schema migration, SQL, Validator, Software, Software quality, Schema (genetic algorithms), Symbolic execution, Business Process Execution Language, Theoretical computer science, Software development, Redundancy (engineering), Database schema, Pairwise comparison, Model checking, Cloud computing, Distributed computing, Semantics (computer science), Process (computing), Unified Modeling Language, Formal methods, Automated reasoning

This is Going to Sound Crazy, But What I.author: William Zhang and Wan Shen Lim and Andrew Pavlo
This is Going to Sound Crazy, But What I.title: This is Going to Sound Crazy, But What If We Used Large Language Models to Boost Automatic Database Tuning Algorithms By Leveraging Prior History? We Will Find Better Configurations More Quickly Than Retraining From Scratch!
This is Going to Sound Crazy, But What I.abstract: Tuning database management systems (DBMSs) is challenging due to trillions of possible configurations and evolving workloads. Recent advances in tuning have led to breakthroughs in optimizing over the possible configurations. However, due to their design and inability to leverage query-level historical insights, existing automated tuners struggle to adapt and re-optimize the DBMS when the environment changes (e.g., workload drift, schema transfer). This paper presents the Booster framework that assists existing tuners in adapting to environment changes (e.g., drift, cross-schema transfer). Booster structures historical artifacts into query-configuration contexts, prompts large language models (LLMs) to suggest configurations for each query based on relevant contexts, and then composes the query-level suggestions into a holistic configuration with beam search. With multiple OLAP workloads, we evaluate Booster's ability to assist different state-of-the-art tuners (e.g., cost-/machine learning-/LLM-based) in adapting to environment changes. By composing recommendations derived from query-level insights, Booster assists tuners in discovering configurations that are up to 74% better and in up to 4.7x less time than the alternative approach of continuing to tune from historical configurations.
This is Going to Sound Crazy, But What I.keywords: Computer science, Booster (rocketry), Leverage (statistics), Database, Workload, Performance tuning, Schema (genetic algorithms), Boosting (machine learning), Database administrator, Online analytical processing, Adapter (computing), Oracle, Crawling, Computer engineering, Data structure, Data modeling, Database schema, Software engineering, Data access, Data mining, User interface, SQL

Enterprise Deep Research: Steerable Mult.author: Akshara Prabhakar and Roshan Ram and Chen, Zixiang and Silvio Savarese and Fengqiu Wang and Caiming Xiong and Huan Wang and Weiran Yao
Enterprise Deep Research: Steerable Mult.title: Enterprise Deep Research: Steerable Multi-Agent Deep Research for Enterprise Analytics
Enterprise Deep Research: Steerable Mult.abstract: As information grows exponentially, enterprises face increasing pressure to transform unstructured data into coherent, actionable insights. While autonomous agents show promise, they often struggle with domain-specific nuances, intent alignment, and enterprise integration. We present Enterprise Deep Research (EDR), a multi-agent system that integrates (1) a Master Planning Agent for adaptive query decomposition, (2) four specialized search agents (General, Academic, GitHub, LinkedIn), (3) an extensible MCP-based tool ecosystem supporting NL2SQL, file analysis, and enterprise workflows, (4) a Visualization Agent for data-driven insights, and (5) a reflection mechanism that detects knowledge gaps and updates research direction with optional human-in-the-loop steering guidance. These components enable automated report generation, real-time streaming, and seamless enterprise deployment, as validated on internal datasets. On open-ended benchmarks including DeepResearch Bench and DeepConsult, EDR outperforms state-of-the-art agentic systems without any human steering. We release the EDR framework and benchmark trajectories to advance research on multi-agent reasoning applications. Code at https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/enterprise-deep-research and Dataset at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Salesforce/EDR-200
Enterprise Deep Research: Steerable Mult.keywords: Computer science, Enterprise software, Enterprise systems engineering, Enterprise information system, Benchmark (surveying), Analytics, Enterprise system, Reflection (computer programming), Enterprise planning system, Enterprise integration, Visualization, Data science, Knowledge management, Software engineering, Enterprise life cycle, Enterprise architecture, Code (set theory), Enterprise modelling, Face (sociological concept), Enterprise data management, Database, Knowledge extraction, Integrated enterprise modeling, Data access, Data visualization, Visual analytics, Data modeling, Semantic reasoner, Artificial intelligence, Key (lock), Information system, Process management, Data mining

A hybrid approach to formulaic alpha dis.author: Shuo Yu and Hongyan Xue and Xiang Ao and Qing He
A hybrid approach to formulaic alpha dis.title: A hybrid approach to formulaic alpha discovery with large language model assistance
A hybrid approach to formulaic alpha dis.abstract: Abstract In the domain of quantitative trading, the imperative is to translate historical financial data into predictive signals, commonly referred to as alpha factors, which serves to anticipate future market trends. Notably, formulaic alphas that are expressible via explicit mathematical formulas are highly sought after by certain investors for better interpretability. The evolving landscape of technology has witnessed the increasing deployment of large language models (LLMs) across various domains, which raises the question of whether LLMs can be effective in the context of formulaic alpha-mining tasks. This paper presents several paradigms aimed at integrating LLMs into the optimization loop of alpha mining, including scenarios where an LLM serves as the sole alpha generator, as well as instances where LLMs enhance existing frameworks. Empirical evaluations on real-world stock data demonstrate significant performance improvements, with our hybrid method achieving an average information coefficient (IC) of 0.0515, a 75% improvement over the baseline — a state-of-the-art reinforcement learning-based framework; backtesting further reveals a cumulative excess return more than double the baseline framework. These results underscore the potential of LLM-enhanced approaches in advancing formulaic alpha discovery and driving innovation in quantitative trading.
A hybrid approach to formulaic alpha dis.keywords: Computer science, Context (archaeology), Alpha (finance), Baseline (sea), Artificial intelligence, Stock market, Software deployment, Machine learning, Domain (mathematical analysis), Financial market, Data science, Stock (firearms), Natural language processing, Econometrics, Language model, Empirical research, Empirical evidence

Implementasi Chatbot Berbasis Aturan unt.author: Surya Rizky Maulana Ibrahim and Dede Handayani
Implementasi Chatbot Berbasis Aturan unt.title: Implementasi Chatbot Berbasis Aturan untuk Layanan Customer Service E-commerce pada Platform WhatsApp
Implementasi Chatbot Berbasis Aturan unt.abstract: The high intensity of repetitive questions regarding product information, order status, and store policies in e-commerce businesses creates an additional workload for customer service and delays responses to customers. This research aims to implement a rule-based chatbot on the WhatsApp platform to automate customer service. The method used is the Waterfall software engineering model with stages of needs analysis, design, implementation, testing, and evaluation. The chatbot was implemented using Python integrated with WhatsApp Business API utilizing quick reply features. Functional testing results on 100 question samples show 87% accuracy. Usability testing using the System Usability Scale (SUS) on 30 users yielded a score of 78.5 (category "Good"). These results indicate that the proposed solution is effective in handling routine inquiries and can reduce customer service operational burden by 40% based on response time measurements. The main limitation lies in handling complex questions that require real-time data checking from external inventory systems.
Implementasi Chatbot Berbasis Aturan unt.keywords: Chatbot, Usability, Computer science, Workload, Service (business), Waterfall model, Customer service, Software, System usability scale, Software engineering, Python (programming language), World Wide Web, Systems development life cycle, Order (exchange)

Aixel: A Unified, Adaptive and Extensibl.author: Meihui Zhang and Liming Wang and Chi Zhang and Zhaojing Luo
Aixel: A Unified, Adaptive and Extensibl.title: Aixel: A Unified, Adaptive and Extensible System for AI-powered Data Analysis
Aixel: A Unified, Adaptive and Extensibl.abstract: A growing trend in modern data analysis is the integration of data management with learning, guided by accuracy, latency, and cost requirements. In practice, applications draw data of different formats from many sources. In the meanwhile, the objectives and budgets change over time. Existing systems handle these applications across databases, analysis libraries, and tuning services. Such fragmentation leads to complex user interaction, limited adaptability, suboptimal performance, and poor extensibility across components. To address these challenges, we present Aixel, a unified, adaptive, and extensible system for AI-powered data analysis. The system organizes work across four layers: application, task, model, and data. The task layer provides a declarative interface to capture user intent, which is parsed into an executable operator plan. An optimizer compiles and schedules this plan to meet specified goals in accuracy, latency, and cost. The task layer coordinates the execution of data and model operators, with built-in support for reuse and caching to improve efficiency. The model layer offers versioned storage for index, metadata, tensors, and model artifacts. It supports adaptive construction, task-aligned drift detection, and safe updates that reuse shared components. The data layer provides unified data management capabilities, including indexing, constraint-aware discovery, task-aligned selection, and comprehensive feature management. With the above designed layers, Aixel delivers a user friendly, adaptive, efficient, and extensible system.
Aixel: A Unified, Adaptive and Extensibl.keywords: Computer science, Extensibility, Executable, Reuse, Layer (electronics), Data management, Task (project management), Data access layer, Data modeling, Distributed computing, Interface (matter), User interface, Database, Software engineering, Data model (GIS), Data type, Task management, Data integration, Feature (linguistics), Component (thermodynamics), Data structure, Data mining, Data integrity, Data access, Feature model, Graphical user interface, Macro, Task analysis, Application layer, Parsing

Robust Clinical Querying with Local LLMs.author: Luka Blašković and Nikola Tanković and Ivan Lorencin and Sandi Baressi Šegota
Robust Clinical Querying with Local LLMs.title: Robust Clinical Querying with Local LLMs: Lexical Challenges in NL2SQL and Retrieval-Augmented QA on EHRs
Robust Clinical Querying with Local LLMs.abstract: Electronic health records (EHRs) are typically stored in relational databases, making them difficult to query for nontechnical users, especially under privacy constraints. We evaluate two practical clinical NLP workflows, natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) for EHR querying and retrieval-augmented generation for clinical question answering (RAG-QA), with a focus on privacy-preserving deployment. We benchmark nine large language models, spanning open-weight options (DeepSeek V3/V3.1, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen2.5-32B, Mixtral-8 × 22B, BioMistral-7B, and GPT-OSS-20B) and proprietary APIs (GPT-4o and GPT-5). The models were chosen to represent a diverse cross-section spanning sparse MoE, dense general-purpose, domain-adapted, and proprietary LLMs. On MIMICSQL (27,000 generations; nine models × three runs), the best NL2SQL execution accuracy (EX) is 66.1% (GPT-4o), followed by 64.6% (GPT-5). Among open-weight models, DeepSeek V3.1 reaches 59.8% EX, while DeepSeek V3 reaches 58.8%, with Llama-3.3-70B at 54.5% and BioMistral-7B achieving only 11.8%, underscoring a persistent gap relative to general-domain benchmarks. We introduce SQL-EC, a deterministic SQL error-classification framework with adjudication, revealing string mismatches as the dominant failure (86.3%), followed by query-join misinterpretations (49.7%), while incorrect aggregation-function usage accounts for only 6.7%. This highlights lexical/ontology grounding as the key bottleneck for NL2SQL in the biomedical domain. For RAG-QA, evaluated on 100 synthetic patient records across 20 questions (54,000 reference–generation pairs; three runs), BLEU and ROUGE-L fluctuate more strongly across models, whereas BERTScore remains high on most, with DeepSeek V3.1 and GPT-4o among the top performers; pairwise t-tests confirm that significant differences were observed among the LLMs. Cost–performance analysis based on measured token usage shows per-query costs ranging from USD 0.000285 (GPT-OSS-20B) to USD 0.005918 (GPT-4o); DeepSeek V3.1 offers the best open-weight cost–accuracy trade-off, and GPT-5 provides a balanced API alternative. Overall, the privacy-conscious RAG-QA attains strong semantic fidelity, whereas the clinical NL2SQL remains brittle under lexical variation. SQL-EC pinpoints actionable failure modes, motivating ontology-aware normalization and schema-linked prompting for robust clinical querying.
Robust Clinical Querying with Local LLMs.keywords: Computer science, SQL, Natural language processing, Relational database, Artificial intelligence, Benchmark (surveying), Bottleneck, Security token, Question answering, Key (lock), Pairwise comparison, Focus (optics), String (physics), Information retrieval, Natural language, Data mining, Training set, Health records, Language model, Data access, Machine learning, Generalization, Data modeling, F1 score

StepFly: Agentic Troubleshooting Guide A.author: Mao, Jiayi and Liqun Li and Yanjie Gao and Peng, Zegang and Shilin He and Chaoyun Zhang and Si Qin and S Khairou Khalid and Qingwei Lin and Saravan Rajmohan and Sitaram Lanka and Dongmei Zhang
StepFly: Agentic Troubleshooting Guide A.title: StepFly: Agentic Troubleshooting Guide Automation for Incident Diagnosis
StepFly: Agentic Troubleshooting Guide A.abstract: Effective incident management in large-scale IT systems relies on troubleshooting guides (TSGs), but their manual execution is slow and error-prone. While recent advances in LLMs offer promise for automating incident management tasks, existing LLM-based solutions lack specialized support for several key challenges, including managing TSG quality issues, interpreting complex control flow, handling data-intensive queries, and exploiting execution parallelism. We first conducted an empirical study on 92 real-world TSGs, and, guided by our findings, we present StepFly, a novel end-to-end agentic framework for troubleshooting guide automation. Our approach features a three-stage workflow: the first stage provides a comprehensive guide together with a tool, TSG Mentor, to assist site reliability engineers (SREs) in improving TSG quality; the second stage performs offline preprocessing using LLMs to extract structured execution directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from unstructured TSGs and to create dedicated Query Preparation Plugins (QPPs); and the third stage executes online using a DAG-guided scheduler-executor framework with a memory system to ensure correct workflow and support parallel execution of independent steps. Our empirical evaluation on a collection of real-world TSGs and incidents demonstrates that StepFly achieves a ~94% success rate on GPT-4.1, outperforming baselines with less time and token consumption. Furthermore, it achieves a remarkable execution time reduction of 32.9% to 70.4% for parallelizable TSGs. Our code and sample data are publicly available at https://github.com/microsoft/StepFly.
StepFly: Agentic Troubleshooting Guide A.keywords: Troubleshooting, Computer science, Workflow, Key (lock), Event (particle physics), Automation, Preprocessor, Software engineering, Incident management, Plug and play, Plug-in, Task (project management), Control (management), Matching (statistics), Quality (philosophy), Empirical research, Operating system, Server, Component (thermodynamics), Unexpected events, Protocol (science), Security token, Uniprocessor system, Timestamp, Database, Identification (biology), Window (computing), Exception handling, Process management, Web application, Workflow management system, Flagging, Point (geometry), Management system, Workflow engine, World Wide Web

RAG-NL2SQL:Implementing the Conversion f.author: Zhihong Xia and Xiankun Zhang and Zhen Pu
RAG-NL2SQL:Implementing the Conversion f.title: RAG-NL2SQL:Implementing the Conversion from Natural Language to Unstructured Database Query Language Based on RAG
RAG-NL2SQL:Implementing the Conversion f.abstract: So far, natural language to structured query language (NL2SQL) conversion has become a critical technology for database access and intelligent question answering. Unstructured databases have gained wide application in data storage for their flexibility. However, the complexity of unstructured databases and the diversity of natural language expressions present significant challenges for existing NL2SQL methods, particularly in semantic understanding and the generation of complex SQL queries. To address these issues, this paper proposes an NL2SQL approach based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which dynamically retrieves relevant contextual information to enhance the input of large language models, thereby improving the comprehension of complex semantics and the accuracy of SQL generation. The results show a promising direction for RAG to enhance the capabilities of current language models, even though their accuracy remains insufficient when used unsupervised. We conducted systematic experiments on public datasets, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms traditional NL2SQL approaches and simple prompting techniques across multiple complex scenarios, exhibiting particularly strong adaptability and accuracy in unstructured data environments.

Logits Replay + MoClip: Stabilized, Low-.author: Qiu, Suming and Liqiang Jing and Zhou, Zhicheng and Junjie Huang and Qiu, Linyuan and Zhijie Sun
Logits Replay + MoClip: Stabilized, Low-.title: Logits Replay + MoClip: Stabilized, Low-Cost Post-Training with Minimal Forgetting
Logits Replay + MoClip: Stabilized, Low-.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often face a trade-off in post-training: improvements on specialized domains frequently come at the expense of general capabilities. Existing solutions attempt to mitigate this tension via regularization, selective parameter updates, or data-centric replay, but each imposes significant costs in computation, data access, or adaptability. Recent work has shown that training signals can be compressed to subsets of logits without severe accuracy loss, suggesting a path toward efficient adaptation. However, naive truncation destabilizes optimization and exacerbates forgetting. We introduce Logits Replay + MoClip, a two-stage framework that compresses supervision in the logit space and stabilizes optimization at the update level. In Stage 0, we record dynamic Top-K token subsets that cover a probability threshold, always including the gold label. In Stage 1, we replay these compact subsets to compute exact renormalized losses, avoiding full softmax computation and implicitly regularizing. To ensure stability, we design MoClip, an optimizer that caps gradient-momentum rotation and applies an arctan2-based rescaling of updates. Empirically, our method improves domain performance on Communication Technology (CT) and NL2SQL tasks while mitigating forgetting on general benchmarks (MMLU, BBH, GPQA, MATH), and reduces training cost by over 40%. Together, these contributions offer a scalable, architecture-agnostic path for domain adaptation of LLMs without sacrificing generalization.

Evaluating NL2SQL via SQL2NL.author: Association for Computational Linguistics 2025 and Oroojlooy, Afshin and Roth, Dan and Safarzadeh, Mohammadtaher
Evaluating NL2SQL via SQL2NL.title: Evaluating NL2SQL via SQL2NL
Evaluating NL2SQL via SQL2NL.abstract: Robustness evaluation applied to linguistic variation is key to understanding the generalization capabilities of Natural Language to SQL (NL2SQL) models, yet existing benchmarks seldom address this factor in a systematic or controlled manner. We propose a novel schema-aligned paraphrasing framework that leverages SQL-to-NL (SQL2NL) to automatically generate semantically equivalent, lexically diverse queries while maintaining alignment with the original schema and intent. This enables the first targeted evaluation of NL2SQL robustness to linguistic variation in isolation---distinct from prior work that primarily investigates ambiguity or schema perturbations. Our analysis reveals that state-of-the-art models are far more brittle than standard benchmarks suggest. For example, LLaMa3.3-70B exhibits a 10.23% drop in execution accuracy (from 77.11% to 66.9%) on paraphrased Spider queries, while LLaMa3.1-8B suffers an even larger drop of nearly 20% (from 62.9% to 42.5%). Smaller models are disproportionately affected. We also find that robustness degradation varies significantly with query complexity, dataset, and domain---highlighting the need for evaluation frameworks that explicitly measure linguistic generalization to ensure reliable performance in real-world settings.
Evaluating NL2SQL via SQL2NL.keywords: Computer science, Ambiguity, Robustness (evolution), Schema (genetic algorithms), Natural language processing, Artificial intelligence, Natural language, Data mining, Computational linguistics, Machine learning, Variation (astronomy), Language understanding

An approach for systematic decomposition.author: Tianle Zhou and Jianhua Xu and Liu, Guanhong and Jiaxiang Liu and Haonan Wang and E. Wu
An approach for systematic decomposition.title: An approach for systematic decomposition of complex llm tasks
An approach for systematic decomposition.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from reliability issues on complex tasks, as existing decomposition methods are heuristic and rely on agent or manual decomposition. This work introduces a novel, systematic decomposition framework that we call Analysis of CONstraint-Induced Complexity (ACONIC), which models the task as a constraint problem and leverages formal complexity measures to guide decomposition. On combinatorial (SAT-Bench) and LLM database querying tasks (Spider), we find that by decomposing the tasks following the measure of complexity, agent can perform considerably better.
An approach for systematic decomposition.keywords: Computer science, Decomposition, Task (project management), Measure (data warehouse), Heuristic, Reliability (semiconductor), Constraint (computer-aided design), Artificial intelligence, Decomposition method (queueing theory), Theoretical computer science, Machine learning, Heuristics, Data mining, Computational complexity theory, Task analysis, Key (lock), Work (physics), Model checking

Natural Language to SQL Queries Using LL.author: G R Abijith and Darwin Raja Kumar R and Derwin Anto A
Natural Language to SQL Queries Using LL.title: Natural Language to SQL Queries Using LLM Models
Natural Language to SQL Queries Using LL.abstract: The increasing reliance on data-driven decision making has made database interaction a critical component in many domains. However, writing Structured Query Language (SQL) queries requires specific technical knowledge, which creates an accessibility barrier for non-technical users. This project proposes a Natural Language to SQL (NL2SQL) system that utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs) to convert user queries expressed in everyday language into valid SQL statements. The system enables users to retrieve information from a database without requiring prior SQL expertise, improving usability and broadening access to data insights.

Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Co.author: Qizheng Zhang and Changran Hu and Shubhangi Upasani and Boyuan Ma and Hong, Fenglu and Vamsidhar Kamanuru and Jay Rainton and Wu Chen and Mengmeng Ji and Hanchen Li and Urmish Thakker and James Zou and Kunle Olukotun
Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Co.title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models
Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Co.abstract: Large language model (LLM) applications such as agents and domain-specific reasoning increasingly rely on context adaptation: modifying inputs with instructions, strategies, or evidence, rather than weight updates. Prior approaches improve usability but often suffer from brevity bias, which drops domain insights for concise summaries, and from context collapse, where iterative rewriting erodes details over time. We introduce ACE (Agentic Context Engineering), a framework that treats contexts as evolving playbooks that accumulate, refine, and organize strategies through a modular process of generation, reflection, and curation. ACE prevents collapse with structured, incremental updates that preserve detailed knowledge and scale with long-context models. Across agent and domain-specific benchmarks, ACE optimizes contexts both offline (e.g., system prompts) and online (e.g., agent memory), consistently outperforming strong baselines: +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance, while significantly reducing adaptation latency and rollout cost. Notably, ACE could adapt effectively without labeled supervision and instead by leveraging natural execution feedback. On the AppWorld leaderboard, ACE matches the top-ranked production-level agent on the overall average and surpasses it on the harder test-challenge split, despite using a smaller open-source model. These results show that comprehensive, evolving contexts enable scalable, efficient, and self-improving LLM systems with low overhead.
Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Co.keywords: Computer science, Context (archaeology), Process (computing), Adaptation (eye), Modular design, Rewriting, Artificial intelligence, Iterative and incremental development, Human–computer interaction, Domain (mathematical analysis), Language model, Natural language, Usability, Context model, Scale (ratio), Natural language understanding, Action (physics), Cognitive science, Natural language processing, Latency (audio), Machine learning, Key (lock)

Improving Code Generation Based on LoRA.author: Yuanfan Zhao
Improving Code Generation Based on LoRA.title: Improving Code Generation Based on LoRA Fine-Tuning
Improving Code Generation Based on LoRA.abstract: With the growing popularity of chatbot programs like ChatGPT and DeepSeek, people are no longer content with merely simple text for the content they generate. With the popularization of the IT and Internet industries, the demand for generating code based on natural language descriptions is increasing day by day. This paper will experimentally demonstrate how fine-tuning the CodeLlama model based on Parameter Efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), especially low-rank adaptive (LoRA) technology, optimizes and improves the generation of Python code, and use authoritative evaluation indicators to prove this point. We will conduct research on code generation by drawing on datasets such as HumanEval and APPs. Then, we will fine-tune the existing baseline model using fine-tuning techniques. We will comprehensively evaluate the generation results before and after fine-tuning using the pass@k metric, plot the results in a chart for comparison, and draw conclusions accordingly. The experimental results show that, on the whole, the Code Llama model before and after LoRA fine-tuning has a significant improvement in the pass rate of the generated code. However, under different indicators, the improvement effect varies slightly. Under the evaluation system of pass@k, the greater the k value, the higher the absolute percentage increase, but the lower the relative percentage increase. Under the condition that other factors remain unchanged, although increasing the rank of the fine-tuning matrix can improve the pass rate of the generated code, the training time will significantly increase, indicating certain limitations.
Improving Code Generation Based on LoRA.keywords: Computer science, Baseline (sea), Popularity, Chart, Code (set theory), Python (programming language), Natural language generation, Rank (graph theory), The Internet, Word error rate, Source code, Web application, Language model, Chatbot, Natural language, Real-time computing, Data mining, Fuzzy logic, Natural language understanding, Artificial intelligence, Machine learning, Mean reciprocal rank, Information retrieval, Code generation

Integrating AI into Urban Decision-Makin.author: Renada Rebhi Abd Alkader and Riyad Ashmeel
Integrating AI into Urban Decision-Makin.title: Integrating AI into Urban Decision-Making: A Case Study of KFUPM Campus
Integrating AI into Urban Decision-Makin.abstract: King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals (KFUPM) currently relies on administrative judgment for urban planning, which may introduce human errors and overlook environmental and social factors. This study examines the potential of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in enhancing urban planning decisions, using Autodesk Forma to analyze solar exposure, wind behavior, and microclimate variations across proposed building sites. The research utilizes an AI-driven site evaluation framework to conduct multi-faceted environmental analyses, including highly predictive wind movement simulations. This approach generated quantifiable metrics essential for sustainable urban design optimization, demonstrating, for example, that one design option achieved 0% uncomfortable pedestrian wind conditions, a key measure of success unattainable through conventional evaluation methods. The findings highlight AI’s role in improving planning accuracy, reducing reliance on static datasets, and supporting data-driven decision-making. However, human expertise remains essential for interpreting AI-generated outputs, ensuring alignment with institutional goals, and validating results. Future research should explore integrating custom datasets for enhanced wind analysis, AI-driven site selection automation, and expert-driven refinements to planning strategies. This study underscores AI’s value in urban planning while emphasizing the need for a hybrid AI-human decision-making framework.

Early combination of albumin with crysta.author: Yuming Teng and Min Li and Mo Yuan
Early combination of albumin with crysta.title: Early combination of albumin with crystalloids administration might not be beneficial for the survival of ischemic stroke patients with sepsis: A retrospective analysis from MIMIC‑IV database
Early combination of albumin with crysta.abstract: The high incidence of sepsis following stroke is a key factor driving increased mortality. Despite the lack of clinical benefit from albumin therapy in stroke patients, emerging data support the use of crystalloid and albumin combination therapy for improving outcomes in sepsis. However, the potential utility of this combination therapy specifically in stroke patients with sepsis has yet to be determined. The objective of this study is to investigate the correlation between the combination of albumin with crystalloids and the 90-day mortality of ischemic stroke patients with sepsis. We included ischemic stroke patients with sepsis from the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care (MIMIC)-IV database. Patients were categorized into the crystalloids group (crystalloids alone) and the combination group. Kaplan-Meier curves were applied to identify the survival probabilities of patients after different therapies for the 90-day. Multivariate COX regression analysis was used to investigate the association between different therapies and 90-days all-cause mortality. The Kaplan-Meier curve showed that patients in the combination group, especially those in the non-early combination group, had worse 90-day survival rate than those in the crystalloids group (all log-rank P < 0.05). In multivariate analyses, early combination therapy showed no significant association with reduced mortality, whereas non-early combination therapy remained independently associated with an increased risk of 90-day all-cause mortality (all P < 0.05). The above results were consistent in the post-PSM analyses and subgroup analyses. In ischemic stroke patients with sepsis, the administration of albumin combined with crystalloids was not associated with improved 90-day survival, regardless of whether the therapy was initiated early or not.
Early combination of albumin with crysta.keywords: Medicine, Sepsis, Stroke (engine), Internal medicine, Retrospective cohort study, Combination therapy, Multivariate analysis, Proportional hazards model, Subgroup analysis, Ischemic stroke, Albumin, Incidence (geometry), Intensive care medicine, Mortality rate, Serum albumin, Survival analysis, Brain ischemia, Survival rate, Surgery, Area under the curve, Severity of illness, Intensive care

Credit card fraud classification using a.author: Kelechi Amamba and Olufemi Oloniluyi and Olayinka Sikiru
Credit card fraud classification using a.booktitle: Journal of Advanced Engineering and Computation
Credit card fraud classification using a.title: Credit card fraud classification using applied machine learning – a comparative study of 24 machine learning algorithms
Credit card fraud classification using a.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive study on credit card fraud detection, addressing the escalating issue of fraudulent activities that significantly impact both financial institutions and consumers. We introduce a novel framework for evaluating the collective performance of diverse machine learning (ML) models—including Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forests, Support Vector Machines, and Neural Networks—using a synthetic dataset carefully constructed to mirror real-world transaction features and behavioral patterns. By applying various sampling strategies to this highly imbalanced dataset and leveraging domain knowledge for feature selection, this study aims to enhance both the accuracy and stability of fraud detection models, while identifying the minimum feature set required for optimal detection speed and efficiency. Our results reveal that algorithms such as Gaussian Naive Bayes, Kernel Naive Bayes, Cubic SVM, and Trilayered Neural Networks each provide strong, balanced performance. Building on these findings, we propose that ensembling these top-performing models could further improve detection rates and reliablity, harnessing their complementary strengths to achieve superior overall performance. This paper underscores the necessity of advanced and integrated ML techniques for robust, timely fraud detection, offering valuable insights for real-time implementation and presenting a comprehensive solution to a pressing financial security challenge.

Cross-Functional Team Collaboration and.booktitle: International Journal of Social Sciences and Economic Management
Cross-Functional Team Collaboration and.title: Cross-Functional Team Collaboration and Project Management in the Automotive Industry
Cross-Functional Team Collaboration and.abstract: With the increasing complexity and internationalization of automotive production and manufacturing, cross-functional team collaboration has become a key factor in driving project success.This paper explores the significance of cross-functional team collaboration in the automotive manufacturing industry and its application in project management, analyzes the main challenges currently faced, and provides corresponding solutions.Research shows that effective cross-functional team collaboration can significantly improve the quality, efficiency, and innovation ability of projects, and plays an important role in the sustainable development and competitiveness enhancement of the automotive manufacturing industry.

Understanding user perceptions of LLM-po.author: Raiymbek Manarbek
Understanding user perceptions of LLM-po.title: Understanding user perceptions of LLM-powered data access for business intelligence: A task-technology fit perspective in fintech
Understanding user perceptions of LLM-po.abstract: Business intelligence faces persistent challenges, with data access remaining concentrated among technical specialists, and nearly 70-80% of projects failing to deliver expected returns. Large Language Models could offer a potential solution via natural language interfaces for data access through SQL translation, potentially eliminating technical barriers. However, technical capability does not guarantee adoption where verification and integration requirements may matter more than accuracy. This research investigates stakeholder perceptions of LLM-powered data access through Task-Technology Fit theory in a fintech startup context. A functional prototype using locally deployed LLMs (Gemma 3:4b via Ollama) was developed and evaluated through 30 queries across complexity levels. Semi-structured interviews with five stakeholders explored perceived fit and adoption barriers. Findings reveal that perceived fit varies substantially by use case. Simple operational queries achieved 83% success with strong acceptance, whilst advanced queries succeeded only 22%, raising verification concerns. Stakeholders valued operational workflows over strategic analytics, particularly multi-transaction lookup addressing immediate friction points. However, standalone architecture emerged as a critical barrier, with stakeholders favouring workflow-integrated deployment. The research extends Task-Technology Fit theory by establishing transparency and verification as core fit dimensions for algorithmic systems. Practically, findings suggest lightweight local LLMs deliver meaningful value for bounded use cases, challenging assumptions that accuracy improvements alone drive enterprise adoption

Web Based Academic Information System fo.author: Dilip Chavan
Web Based Academic Information System fo.booktitle: International Journal on Advanced Computer Theory and Engineering
Web Based Academic Information System fo.title: Web Based Academic Information System for Academic Communication
Web Based Academic Information System fo.abstract: The information system for academic communication uses advanced collaborative tools for effectiveness in handling the overall activities. The artificial intelligence algorithms incorporated along with various techniques to achieve the effectiveness in obtaining the goal oriented tasks. The typical academic activities like sharing of resources and academic calendar amongst the entities such as faculties and students in collaboration for achievement of desired goals. In the era of technology, online platforms are in a position to facilitate smooth communication between learners and instructors. This paper assesses the Intolerant platform, as detailed in the given html wireframe, evaluating its functionality and design in a bid to comprehend its potential in enhancing learning interactions. The research identifies major features such as smooth messaging, assignment management facilitation, and monitoring of academic progress.

ІНТЕЛЕКТУАЛЬНІ ПІДХОДИ ДО АВТОМАТИЗАЦІЇ.author: Р. Б. Юринець
ІНТЕЛЕКТУАЛЬНІ ПІДХОДИ ДО АВТОМАТИЗАЦІЇ.booktitle: Таврійський науковий вісник Серія Технічні науки
ІНТЕЛЕКТУАЛЬНІ ПІДХОДИ ДО АВТОМАТИЗАЦІЇ.title: ІНТЕЛЕКТУАЛЬНІ ПІДХОДИ ДО АВТОМАТИЗАЦІЇ УПРАВЛІННЯ МЕТАДАНИМИ У DATA LAKES: ПОРІВНЯЛЬНИЙ АНАЛІЗ СУЧАСНИХ МОДЕЛЕЙ
ІНТЕЛЕКТУАЛЬНІ ПІДХОДИ ДО АВТОМАТИЗАЦІЇ.abstract: У статті представлено дослідження сучасних підходів до управління метаданими у Data Lakes (озера даних), що функціонують як масштабовані архітектури для зберігання та обробки гетерогенних даних. Узагальнено розвиток зональної моделі Data Lake та показано, що якість аналітичних процесів значною мірою визначається повнотою та узгодженістю метаданих. Детально розглянуто класифікацію технічних, операційних, предметних і семантичних метаданих та їхню роль у забезпеченні відтворюваності, інтерпретації та контекстуального доступу до даних. Проаналізовано три основні групи моделей: ручні та засновані на правилах, онтологічні та семантичні, а також ML / AI-орієнтовані. Показано, що традиційні моделі забезпечують структурованість, але обмежені в масштабованості й адаптивності до еволюції схем. Семантичні підходи підвищують узгодженість та інтероперабельність за рахунок онтологій і механізмів semantic mapping (семантичне відображення), однак потребують значних ресурсів на підтримку концептуальних структур. ML-моделі забезпечують автоматичне виявлення закономірностей, типізацію та класифікацію, демонструючи високу продуктивність у потокових і динамічних середовищах, хоча зберігають проблему непрозорості та потребують контролю якості. Наведено систему критеріїв оцінювання моделей, що охоплює рівень автоматизації, семантичну насиченість, масштабованість, здатність підтримувати еволюцію даних, інтегрованість із управління даними (data governance) та lineage (трасування потоків і трансформацій), а також зручність взаємодії для користувачів. Порівняльний аналіз показує, що жоден підхід не усуває всіх обмежень: ручні моделі ефективні для стабільних корпоративних середовищ, семантичні забезпечують концептуальну узгодженість, а ML-рішення оптимальні для високодинамічних сценаріїв. Узагальнення результатів вказує, що ефективні стратегії управління метаданими потребують поєднання автоматизації, семантичної інтерпретації та механізмів контролю якості, що визначає подальші напрями розвитку інтелектуальних підходів у Data Lakes.
ІНТЕЛЕКТУАЛЬНІ ПІДХОДИ ДО АВТОМАТИЗАЦІЇ.keywords: Computer science, Genetic data, Field (mathematics), Population, Data set, Artificial intelligence, Data mining, Term (time), Raw data, Linked data

Design strategies and application progre.title: Design strategies and application progress of covalent organic frameworks in photocatalytic oxidation reactions
Design strategies and application progre.abstract: production, and efficient degradation of recalcitrant pollutants. The application scope is further extended to biomedical fields, leveraging ROS for antibacterial therapy and photodynamic effects. Finally, we discuss prevailing challenges in quantitative ROS detection, operational stability, and scalable synthesis, while outlining future opportunities in machine-learning-guided design and tandem catalytic systems. This review aims to establish fundamental design principles for the next generation of COF-based photocatalysts, bridging molecular-level engineering to macroscopic oxidative efficacy.
